title: Evidence Fidelity and Timebound Authority Playbook
preview:
Purpose

This playbook defines the Evidence Fidelity model and the Timebound Authority framework used at Redwood Inference to ensure that all access grants, privileged actions, and investigative artifacts are recorded, verifiable, and retained according to risk, data residency, and legal requirements. It descr...
text:
Purpose
------
This playbook defines the Evidence Fidelity model and the Timebound Authority framework used at Redwood Inference to ensure that all access grants, privileged actions, and investigative artifacts are recorded, verifiable, and retained according to risk, data residency, and legal requirements. It describes operational procedures for granting temporary authority, capturing high-fidelity evidence during normal operations and incidents, and closing exceptions with compensating controls.

Scope
-----
Applies to: all engineers, SREs, security, platform, customer-facing success teams, and any third-party operator who requests or holds elevated privileges to infrastructure, model-serving control planes, and customer-managed private deployments.

Key definitions
---------------
- Evidence fidelity: a 3-point score (LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH) representing the completeness, provenance, an...

title: Control Exception Evidence Register Template

This document defines a standardized Control Exception Evidence Register (CEER) and a corresponding template for packaging the evidence required when approving, exercising, and periodically reviewing exceptions to security and compliance controls. The CEER bridges RBAC & auth exception records, audit-log ...
text:
Overview:

This document defines a standardized Control Exception Evidence Register (CEER) and a corresponding template for packaging the evidence required when approving, exercising, and periodically reviewing exceptions to security and compliance controls. The CEER bridges RBAC & auth exception records, audit-log artifacts, data residency/retention constraints, Secure SDLC implications, incident-response hooks, and the compliance evidence workflow used by auditors and security reviewers.

Scope:

- Applies to any formal exception or temporary deviation from documented security controls across Redwood Inference services, including but not limited to RBAC policy relaxations, cross-region data access, altered retention settings, or third-party connector exceptions.
- Intended audience: security reviewers, risk owners, engineering owners, compliance, audit, and SRE.

Key objectives:

1....

title: Privacy-aware Audit Redaction and Evidence Handoff Standards
preview:
Summary:
This page defines Redwood Inference’s standards for privacy-aware audit log redaction, pseudonymization, and the secure handoff of evidence to compliance, legal, and incident response teams. The goal is to preserve forensic utility while minimizing exposure of sensitive customer data and ensuring repeatable...
text:
Summary:
This page defines Redwood Inference’s standards for privacy-aware audit log redaction, pseudonymization, and the secure handoff of evidence to compliance, legal, and incident response teams. The goal is to preserve forensic utility while minimizing exposure of sensitive customer data and ensuring repeatable, auditable evidence packaging for regulatory review.

Scope:
- Applies to all production audit logs generated by Redwood services, edge collectors, and gateway proxies.
- Covers automated redaction rules, manual sanitization workflows, evidence packaging, transport, and retention labeling.
- Does not replace data-residency policies; see linked residency controls for region-specific handling.

Principles:
1) Forensic first: retain sufficient context to reconstruct actions (timestamps, actor id hash, operation, resource id hash), while removing or tokenizing direct identifie...

text:
## Purpose
This standard defines when Security & Compliance (S&C) sign-off is required for customer deployments (Hosted, Dedicated, and Private) and what artifacts must be provided to obtain approval.

## When security sign-off is required
S&C sign-off is required for production changes that:
1. Introduce or change authentication/authorization mechanisms (SSO, JWT validation, mTLS, service-to-service auth).
2. Change RBAC defaults, permission scopes, or privileged service accounts.
3. Change audit logging behavior, retention, log schemas, or delivery guarantees.
4. Change cryptographic material handling (key rotation, KMS/HSM integrations, cert management).
5. Introduce new third-party images/dependencies into the customer environment.
6. Change network boundaries that could affect data exfiltration controls (ingress/egress, proxies, allowlists).

## Required artifacts (minimum)
Provi...

title: Access activation and delegation workflow
preview:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes Redwood Inference's standardized access activation and delegation workflow for short-lived privileged access. It ties RBAC, audit logging, data residency constraints, secure SDLC controls, incident response handoffs, and compliance evidence collection into a single lifecycle...
text:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes Redwood Inference's standardized access activation and delegation workflow for short-lived privileged access. It ties RBAC, audit logging, data residency constraints, secure SDLC controls, incident response handoffs, and compliance evidence collection into a single lifecycle. Purpose: minimize blast radius from elevated access, ensure auditable approvals and evidence, and enable timely emergency access in incidents while preserving regulatory constraints.

Scope
-----
Applies to: engineers, platform engineers, SREs, security responders, and 3rd-party contractors who require temporary elevation (examples: admin, model-promotion, debug, oncall-runbook-access).
Systems in scope: authz-service, access-broker, evidence-orchestrator, audit-pipeline, KMS, Vault, upstream identity provider (IdP).
Exclusions: standard user-level no-elevation tasks (han...

## Summary
This page defines how **audit logs** and **operational observability logs** are produced, stored, and exported in **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem). It clarifies:
- What gets logged where (control plane, data plane, installer/upgrade controller).
- How audit logs relate to (and must be separated from) operational logs.
- Minimum supported retention defaults and recommended customer retention.
- Redaction and sensitive-data handling guidance, including what Redwood components will never log.

This guidance applies to the **Private observability minimum pack** and is intended for customer platform/security teams, Redwood SRE/Support, and Security/Compliance reviewers.

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **recommended IAM posture** for customers deploying **Redwood Private** using the **AWS VPC Terraform modules (alpha)**. It covers:
- Which IAM roles/policies the modules **create** vs **expect**.
- **Least-privilege** recommendations for Terraform and installer operations.
- Mandatory use of **permission boundaries** (and why).
- How customer IAM integrates with `redwood-private-installer` workflows.

The intent is to reduce pilot risk (over-permissioned roles, accidental public access, unclear separation of duties) while keeping the alpha modules usable across common enterprise IAM setups.

---

## Scope and assumptions
**In scope (alpha):**
- IAM scaffolding to run Terraform modules safely in a customer AWS account.
- A dedicated **installer execution role** for `redwood-private-installer` (or an equivalent customer-run automation) with a **permissi...

title: Private VPC logging defaults and SIEM hooks (Terraform modules alpha)
preview:
## Overview
This page defines Redwood-validated logging defaults for the AWS VPC footprint created by the **VPC Terraform modules (alpha)** used for Redwood Private deployments. The goal is to provide a secure, auditable baseline that:
- Enables incident investigation and compliance evidence collection (network path...
text:
## Overview
This page defines Redwood-validated logging defaults for the AWS VPC footprint created by the **VPC Terraform modules (alpha)** used for Redwood Private deployments. The goal is to provide a secure, auditable baseline that:
- Enables incident investigation and compliance evidence collection (network pathing, egress controls, endpoint reachability).
- Minimizes surprise cost/volume while remaining extensible for enterprise SIEMs.
- Keeps logs **in customer-owned accounts and storage by default** (Redwood does not require access to customer network logs for standard support).

This document covers:
- VPC Flow Logs (required)
- DNS query logging (recommended; where applicable)
- Load balancer logs (optional but recommended)
- VPC endpoint telemetry and CloudTrail expectations (adjacent controls)
- Recommended destinations, encryption, retention, and SIEM export patterns

## S...

text:
h2. Summary
QuantaLedger requested strict US-only residency for a planned Redwood Private (VPC) deployment. Redwood Inference approved a limited, time-bounded exception allowing an EU-based (contracted) professional services resource to access *deployment logs and operational metadata* during the initial rollout period.

h2. Customer requirement
* US-only serving and storage for all inference traffic and operational logs.
* Customer has an EU-based subcontractor assisting with rollout and requests Redwood allow that person to participate in troubleshooting.

h2. Approved exception
* Exception type: Out-of-region *human access* to operational metadata
* Serving/storage region remains: us-east (US only)
* Access location: EU (contractor)
* Duration: 2025-06-05 through 2025-07-31
* Scope:
 * Contractor may access: Kubernetes event logs, rollout status, health checks, request metadata (id...

## Overview
This document specifies the **audit log events** emitted for **Hosted canary rollout actions** in Redwood Console / Platform Control Plane. These events support:
- Customer governance and change control (who changed what, when, and why)
- Incident investigations and root cause analysis
- Compliance evidence (SOX/SOC2/ISO-style change management)
- SIEM ingestion/export with stable schemas and retention guarantees

This spec covers:
1) Event taxonomy (names, when emitted)
2) Standard event envelope fields
3) Rollout-specific fields and diff format
4) Retention and access expectations
5) SIEM export considerations
6) Examples

### In scope
- Hosted routes canary rollout workflow actions (create/start/step/pause/resume/rollback)
- Approvals (human approval gates)
- Policy decision enforcement outcomes for rollout actions
- Auto-stop / auto-rollback actions triggered ...

text:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines the **canonical audit event schema v2** used across Redwood Inference products (Hosted, Dedicated) and provides **mapping guidance** for common SIEM destinations in the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, and S3 delivery).

**Goals**
- Provide a stable, versioned schema for audit events suitable for: incident response, compliance evidence, and enterprise SIEM ingestion.
- Ensure a consistent set of identifiers for deduplication and correlation (event_id, request_id/trace_id).
- Ensure PII minimization and predictable field semantics across services.

**Non-goals**
- This document does not define exporter delivery semantics (see ADR: delivery semantics + idempotency).
- This document does not define retention/backfill responsibilities (see policy: retention vs export responsibilities).

---

Audit Event Schem...

preview:

text:
## 1. Purpose
This evidence pack provides auditor-ready artifacts for Redwood Inference’s **Hosted data residency enforcement** capability (“region pinning”). It is designed for:
- SOC 2 Type II evidence requests (Security, Availability, Confidentiality)
- ISO 27001 / ISO 27701 evidence requests where applicable
- Enterprise customer security questionnaires (data location, geo-fencing, auditability)

This pack focuses on **Hosted API** enforcement and notes alignment for **Dedicated** where behavior differs.

## 2. Control objective (plain language)
Redwood Inference ensures that when a customer configures an allowed set of regions for Hosted API (and a primary region), Redwood:
1) Evaluates residency policy deterministically at request time.
2) Routes and processes customer content only within the configured allowed region set.
3) Blocks requests that would violate policy using a con...

text:
## Summary
This document records the Security & Compliance review for the onboarding telemetry used by the **Guided Onboarding + First Production Launch checklist** in the Redwood Console. It covers:
- Data classification and permitted identifiers
- PII avoidance rules and validation expectations
- Retention and deletion policy (raw vs derived)
- Regional data residency constraints (US/EU) and cross-region access
- Access controls and audit considerations

Decision outcome: **Approved** for production use under the constraints and retention controls below.

## Background
The onboarding checklist relies on event instrumentation across the API gateway/control plane and limited Console client hints. Telemetry is used to:
- Compute checklist step state (e.g., “API key created”, “first successful request”, “first streaming response”)
- Produce onboarding KPIs (TTFS/TTFP, funnel drop-off)
-...

text:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines the **audit events** required for the Dedicated **Burst Capacity** feature. The intent is to ensure that:
- All **customer-impacting configuration changes** are attributable to an actor, traceable to a request, and available for compliance evidence.
- Any **administrative override** (including emergency actions) is recorded with sufficient context (who/what/why/when).
- Audit logs are consistent across control plane services and are safe to expose to customers where applicable.

This spec is used by:
- Platform Control Plane engineering (event production)
- Security engineering (schema, access controls, retention)
- SRE (incident forensics)
- CS/Support (customer explanations)
- Legal/Finance (contractual statements, dispute resolution)

## 2. Scope
### In scope
Audit events for:
- Tenant-level burst configuration changes (enable/disable, caps/limit...

title: RBAC v2 Migration and Backwards Compatibility Plan
preview:
## Overview
This page defines the migration plan from RBAC v1 (coarse-grained roles and implicit access paths) to RBAC v2 (fine-grained permissions with explicit scopes and deny-by-default enforcement). The plan is designed to:

- Prevent customer breakage during staged rollout (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).
- Provid...
text:
## Overview
This page defines the migration plan from RBAC v1 (coarse-grained roles and implicit access paths) to RBAC v2 (fine-grained permissions with explicit scopes and deny-by-default enforcement). The plan is designed to:

- Prevent customer breakage during staged rollout (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).
- Provide deterministic authorization behavior with measurable deltas between v1 and v2.
- Offer safe rollback paths and clear operator workflows.
- Produce evidence-friendly artifacts for enterprise audits (SOC2) and customer security reviews.

**Executive sponsors / owners**
- Product: Naomi Feldman
- Security Engineering: Marcus Lin
- Platform Engineering: Priya Natarajan
- Policy engine: Jordan Lee
- Authn/Authz integration: Kaitlyn Nguyen
- Gateway enforcement: Amara Diallo
- Control plane adoption: Ethan Park

---

## Background (RBAC v1 → v2)

## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s policy for **what may and may not appear in audit events**, including:
- Allowed fields and data classes
- Redaction/minimization rules for potentially sensitive fields
- Prohibited payloads (examples included)
- A review checklist for adding or changing audit event types

This policy applies to all audit events emitted by Redwood services (control plane, console, API gateway, exporter services) and to all delivery modes (hosted, dedicated). For the SIEM-ready audit export beta, this policy is part of the security sign-off gate.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Audit events defined by the canonical audit schema (see related page: Audit Event Schema v2)
- Audit events exported to Splunk/Datadog/S3 via the audit exporter
- Audit events stored/queried internally for security investigations

**Out of scope**

- **Evidence-friendly** (SOC 2 / enterprise questionnaires)

text:
## Overview
This page defines the audit log schema extension for **authorization (authz) decision context** as part of the RBAC v2 rollout. The goal is to make authorization decisions:
- **Evidence-friendly** (SOC 2 / enterprise questionnaires)
- **Operationally useful** (debug “why denied?”, incident response, change correlation)
- **Consistent across deployments** (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)

This schema is used by:
- Control Plane services emitting audit events
- API Gateway (where it emits audit events for gateway-mediated actions)
- The audit-log exporter (SIEM forwarding)

Non-goal: this document does not define the full RBAC model. It only defines what we log about the decision.

---

## Design principles
1) **Deny-by-default evidence**: for any security-relevant action, we must be able to show that an authz decision was evaluated and enforced.
2) **Human-readable + machine-pa...

title: RBAC v2 Permission Inventory & Classification (Canonical Catalog)
preview:

It is used to:

- Map **product actions (endpoints/jobs/Console workflows)** to permissions.
- Identify **gaps** (missing checks, missing permissions, in...
text:
## Purpose
This page is the **canonical permission catalog** for Redwood Inference RBAC v2.

It is used to:
- Standardize **permission naming** and **sensitivity classification**.
- Map **product actions (endpoints/jobs/Console workflows)** to permissions.
- Identify **gaps** (missing checks, missing permissions, inconsistent error semantics).
- Provide a source of truth for **role template generation** and **CI enforcement**.

> Ownership note: Security owns the catalog format and tiering. Each domain team owns correctness for their mapped actions and coverage.

---

## Definitions
- **Permission**: A named capability that may be granted via roles and bindings.
- **Action**: A concrete operation (API endpoint, internal RPC, background job) that must be protected by a permission.
- **Resource**: The object being acted upon (org/project/environment/deployment/key/etc.).
- **Scope**: Th...

title: RBAC v2 Design Overview (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)
preview:
## Summary
RBAC v2 is Redwood Inference’s fine-grained authorization model designed to support regulated enterprise workloads across Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments. This document describes RBAC v2 goals, primitives, evaluation flow, storage model, caching strategy, and rollout ...
text:
## Summary
RBAC v2 is Redwood Inference’s fine-grained authorization model designed to support regulated enterprise workloads across Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments. This document describes RBAC v2 goals, primitives, evaluation flow, storage model, caching strategy, and rollout plan.

RBAC v2 is the authorization layer. Authentication (API keys, SSO/SAML/OIDC, service identities) remains unchanged, but all security-relevant actions must map to an explicit permission and be deny-by-default.

## Goals
1. Least-privilege authorization with a canonical permission catalog covering the Redwood product surface area (API Gateway, Console, Control Plane services, Dedicated/Private admin operations).
2. Consistent enforcement and error semantics across:
 - API Gateway (edge enforcement)
 - Control Plane services (authoritative enforcement)
 - Console/admin ...

title: RBAC v2 Role Templates and Permission Matrix
preview:
## Summary
This page defines Redwood’s **built-in RBAC v2 role templates** and the **explicit permission matrix** backing each template. The intent is to provide:
- Strong default roles for enterprise deployments (Hosted, Dedicated, and Private).
- Clear **separation of duties** (Security vs Billing vs Operations).
...
text:
## Summary
This page defines Redwood’s **built-in RBAC v2 role templates** and the **explicit permission matrix** backing each template. The intent is to provide:
- Strong default roles for enterprise deployments (Hosted, Dedicated, and Private).
- Clear **separation of duties** (Security vs Billing vs Operations).
- A predictable starting point for customers, Solutions Engineering, and Support.

These templates are shipped as part of the **policy bundle** and can be assigned at the appropriate scope (Org / Project / Environment / Cluster). Customers may also create custom roles, but the templates below are the recommended baseline.

## Design principles
1. **Deny by default.** Role templates grant only what is explicitly listed.
2. **Separation of duties.** No single non-admin role should cover identity + billing + sensitive security operations.
3. **Auditability first.** Permissions...

title: Break-glass access (RBAC v2): emergency admin procedure and controls
preview:
## Overview
Break-glass access is a controlled, time-bound mechanism to temporarily grant elevated privileges to resolve an incident, restore service, or perform a customer-authorized emergency operation when normal access paths (standard RBAC roles, delegated admin workflows, or automation) are insufficient.

This ...
text:
## Overview
Break-glass access is a controlled, time-bound mechanism to temporarily grant elevated privileges to resolve an incident, restore service, or perform a customer-authorized emergency operation when normal access paths (standard RBAC roles, delegated admin workflows, or automation) are insufficient.

This page defines:
- When break-glass is allowed vs prohibited
- The approval workflow (two-person rule) and emergency exceptions
- How time-bound elevation is implemented in RBAC v2
- Required logging, alerting, and evidence artifacts
- Post-use review requirements and follow-ups

Break-glass is designed to be compatible across **Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments, with deployment-specific operational details called out below.

---

## Goals
1. **Restore or protect service quickly** during incidents while maintaining strong controls.
2. **Deny-by-default**...

title: Secure SDLC Addendum: Template Dependency & Supply Chain Policy
preview:
## Purpose
This document is a Secure SDLC policy addendum for **Redwood Quickstart Templates** ("templates"), including:
- GitHub template repositories (e.g., `redwood-quickstarts`)
- Template CLI/scaffolder output (generated projects)
- Example apps shipped alongside templates when they share the same release proce...
text:
## Purpose
This document is a Secure SDLC policy addendum for **Redwood Quickstart Templates** ("templates"), including:
- GitHub template repositories (e.g., `redwood-quickstarts`)
- Template CLI/scaffolder output (generated projects)
- Example apps shipped alongside templates when they share the same release process

Templates are intentionally copy/pasted into customer environments. That makes them a **supply-chain multiplier**: a single insecure default or compromised dependency can propagate widely. This policy sets minimum requirements for dependency pinning, vulnerability scanning, and safe handling of secrets and sample data.

## Scope
### In scope
- Template source repos maintained by Redwood Inference (Python and TypeScript initially)
- Build/test/deploy artifacts and workflows in those repos (CI, Dockerfiles, GitHub Actions)
- Sample data and configuration included in templ...

## Overview
This document defines the audit logging requirements and event schema for **Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools**. It covers:
- Event types and semantics for actions: **create/update/promote/abort/hold/force overrides**
- Required fields for compliance, forensic review, and customer change management
- RBAC requirements (who can do what, and what must be logged)
- Retention and export (internal UI + customer export + SIEM)
- Example event payloads

This spec applies to all deployment modes of Dedicated pools (single-region and multi-region), and to all rollout targets (model version, runtime build, routing policy, config changes).

## Goals
1. **Deterministic & auditable change history**: A human reviewer can reconstruct what changed, who initiated it, when it took effect, what gates were evaluated, and why the system promoted/aborted.

text:
## Purpose
This page is the published internal overview of **Retention Controls** for Redwood Inference. It describes:
- Supported **data classes** and what they mean at Redwood
- How retention differs by **product tier** (Hosted API / Dedicated / Private)
- **Default retention periods** and safe-guardrails (min/max TTL)
- **Behavioral semantics** (TTL vs purge, deletion verification, backups, legal hold)
- Links to the authoritative schema, standards, runbooks, and evidence pack

Retention Controls are part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation program and are used to answer customer security reviews, configure customer environments, and generate the baseline Compliance Reporting Pack.

---

## Scope and definitions
### What “retention” means at Redwood
Retention is the customer-configurable policy that determines how long Redwood systems keep specific classes of data **...

title: Baseline Compliance Reporting Pack (Customer + Internal Audit)

text:
## Purpose
The Baseline Compliance Reporting Pack (“Compliance Pack”) is a standardized, on-demand evidence bundle for security reviews and audits. It is designed to reduce ad-hoc evidence gathering by providing consistent, repeatable exports covering:
- Retention controls (configuration + change history)
- Audit logging coverage + time-window exports
- Data residency and processing regions summary
- Access control / RBAC configuration summary
- Evidence collection checklist aligned to SOC 2 / ISO-style expectations

This page defines the minimum content (“baseline”), data sources, output formats, and intended audiences. The baseline is deliberately conservative: it includes what most enterprise customers request during procurement/security review while minimizing sensitive internal-only details.

---

## Audience and distribution
### Primary audiences
1) **Customer Security/Complianc...

## Purpose
This page defines the standard, repeatable **audit log export report templates** used for (a) customer security reviews and (b) internal audits / incident support. Each template specifies:
- Required fields
- Recommended filters (event types, actors, outcomes)
- Default time windows
- Output formats

These templates are implemented by the **audit-log-exporter** and are referenced by the baseline Compliance Reporting Pack.

## Scope
**In scope**: exports from the centralized audit log store for Redwood API / Dedicated / Private control plane events where available.

**Out of scope** (handled elsewhere): request/response logs, application traces/metrics, customer data payloads.

## Definitions
- **Actor**: the identity that initiated the action (human user, service account, automated system).

## Purpose
This page defines the repeatable, customer-safe procedure to generate and validate evidence for:
- Retention policy configuration (current state + change history)

This procedure is used for:

## Purpose
This document assesses security, reliability, and compliance risks introduced by the Retention Controls feature set (customer-configurable TTLs, purge semantics, legal hold, and verification) and defines required mitigations, monitoring, and residual risks.

text:
## 1. Purpose
This policy defines when and how Redwood Inference applies (a) **legal holds**, (b) **incident-related holds**, and (c) **retention exceptions** to standard data retention behavior. It also specifies the required **approvals**, **documentation**, and **audit trail** for any hold or exception.

This document is intended to be customer-ready in concept (what we do and why), but contains internal process and tooling references. Share externally only after Security & Legal review.

## 2. Scope
This policy applies to all Redwood Inference product tiers (**Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, **Private**) and all Redwood-managed environments. For Private deployments with customer-managed infrastructure, the same controls apply where supported; some enforcement steps may be performed by the customer with Redwood guidance.

text:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s internal policy for **Hosted API data residency** and the required **engineering and operational controls** used to enforce it. It is written to:
- Provide a clear, auditable statement of what residency guarantees are provided for Hosted API.
- Define enforcement boundaries (what counts as “customer content” and what must remain in-region).
- Specify required controls across routing, fallback behavior, logging/telemetry, and background processing.
- Map controls to common compliance frameworks (SOC 2 and ISO/IEC 27001) for audit narratives.

This policy supports the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative: **Hosted data residency enforcement (region pinning)**.

## 2. Scope
### 2.1 In scope
- **Redwood Hosted API** request handling for text generation/chat, embeddings, reranking, and streaming/non-streaming variant...

title: Authorization Safeguards and Compliance Workflow for Inference Systems
preview:
Overview

This runbook defines an integrated, end-to-end workflow for authorization (RBAC/attribute-based checks), audit logging, data residency validation, secure-SDLC gating, incident response interplay, compliance evidence packaging, and risk/exception handling for Redwood Inference model-serving and control-plan...
text:
Overview

This runbook defines an integrated, end-to-end workflow for authorization (RBAC/attribute-based checks), audit logging, data residency validation, secure-SDLC gating, incident response interplay, compliance evidence packaging, and risk/exception handling for Redwood Inference model-serving and control-plane systems. It is intended for cross-functional use by Security, SRE, Platform, and Product teams responsible for secure model deployments and tenant isolation.

Goals

- Ensure authorization decisions are evaluated and logged with sufficient forensic detail to support audits and incident investigation.
- Prevent unauthorized cross-residency inference or data access by combining policy checks (RBAC/ABAC), residency metadata, and runtime enforcement.
- Provide a standardized evidence bundle and manifest that maps policy decisions to artifact hashes for regulatory review.

preview:

The policy is evaluated in the **control plane** and enforced by data-plane purge/TTL jobs. Policy changes ...
text:
## Overview
This page defines the **Retention Policy** payload schema used by the control plane, the **validation rules** enforced at write-time (API + Console), and tier-specific **min/max TTL** constraints.

The policy is evaluated in the **control plane** and enforced by data-plane purge/TTL jobs. Policy changes are audited, versioned, and exportable for compliance reporting.

**In scope for v1/v1.1:**
- Request/response logs (where logging is enabled)
- Prompts/completions *where stored* (e.g., debugging capture, eval artifacts)
- Audit logs
- Traces and metrics retention for platform telemetry
- Artifacts (eval datasets, red-team prompt sets) where applicable

---

## Terminology
- **Data class**: a named category of stored data subject to a retention rule (e.g., `audit_logs`).
- **TTL**: time-to-live. After TTL elapses, records become eligible for deletion.
- **Retention rule**:...

## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference guidance for **backup, retention, and evidentiary handling** of **audit logs** in **Redwood Private** deployments (VPC and on-prem, including air-gapped). It is intended for:

title: Known issue: Audit log missing events in some Redwood Private deployments (IR-2025-017)
preview:
## Summary
We have identified a known issue in certain **Redwood Private** deployments where **audit log events may be dropped** under sustained downstream backpressure (e.g., slow HTTP sink, temporarily unavailable collector).

This issue is tracked under **IR-2025-017** and primarily affects the audit event routin...
text:
## Summary
We have identified a known issue in certain **Redwood Private** deployments where **audit log events may be dropped** under sustained downstream backpressure (e.g., slow HTTP sink, temporarily unavailable collector).

This issue is tracked under **IR-2025-017** and primarily affects the audit event routing component ("audit-event-router").

## Customer impact
The primary risk is **incomplete audit trails** for a time window. When an event is dropped, it is not recoverable from the router layer.

Reported-by (confirmed):
- HelioPay (Private, us-east)
- AcmeBank (Private, eu-west)
- Globex (Private, sink: HTTP push)
- Umbrella Bio (Private, sink: HTTP push)

Unconfirmed reports (needs timestamps / verification):
- Northwind Health (Private)

## Symptoms
- Audit export stream shows fewer events than expected
- Gaps cluster around peak traffic windows
- No clear customer-visibl...

text:
# Summary
This page provides (1) the standard template for a third-party model risk review, and (2) an example filled out for a recent Hosted API catalog launch.

# Template (required sections)
## Model identity
- Upstream name + version tag
- Source URL
- Proposed Redwood model ID

## Provenance
- Download location
- sha256 hashes for: weights, tokenizer files, config
- Notes on who performed download + when

## Supply chain checks
- Malware scan results
- Container/build environment scan results
- Any scripts used for conversion/quantization and their repo + reviewer

## License review
- License name and link
- Redistribution: yes/no/unclear
- Required restrictions text (if any)

## Policy alignment
- Any elevated abuse risk
- Whether additional staged rollout is required

## Decision
- approved | approved-with-restrictions | blocked
- Approver name + date

text:
# Purpose
This page defines the security and compliance review required before Redwood can ship a third-party model in the Hosted API catalog.

# Scope
Applies to any new upstream weights/tokenizer/artifacts that Redwood will host or make available via the Hosted API.

# Review owner
- Security & Compliance (Risk & Exceptions)

# Inputs required from requester
Provide in the model intake ticket:
- Upstream source (repo URL, commit/tag, download location)
- License and redistribution terms
- Known upstream maintainers and any vendor relationship
- Any customer constraints (data residency, regulated workloads)

# Required checks (all must be completed)
## 1) Artifact provenance and integrity
- Verify checksums for downloaded artifacts
- Record the exact upstream tag/commit and file hashes
- Store hashes in the model registry change description

## 2) Supply chain scanning
- Scan contain...

This page defines *security and compliance requirements* for exporting audit logs from *Redwood Private* to *customer-managed storage* (typically object storage). It is used by Engineering to implement compliant sinks and by Solutions/CS to validate customer configurations.

h2. Definitions

text:
h1. Purpose
This page defines *security and compliance requirements* for exporting audit logs from *Redwood Private* to *customer-managed storage* (typically object storage). It is used by Engineering to implement compliant sinks and by Solutions/CS to validate customer configurations.

h2. Definitions
*Audit logs:* security-relevant records of administrative and control-plane actions within Redwood Private.
*Customer-managed storage:* an account/project owned by the customer (e.g., AWS S3, GCS).

h2. Requirements (MUST)
# *No long-lived static credentials* (access keys) are permitted for audit log export.
# Export auth MUST be via short-lived identity (AWS AssumeRole / GCP Workload Identity Federation).
# Exported objects MUST be encrypted at rest.
## If the sink supports SSE-KMS / CMEK, we MUST support customer-provided KMS key identifiers.
# Exporter MUST provide *at-least-once del...

title: Data residency exception record: NordBank AB (EU-only with temporary US failover)
preview:
h2. Summary
NordBank AB requested an exception to their EU-only data residency requirement to support disaster recovery testing and failover. Redwood Inference approved a *time-bounded* exception to route inference traffic to a US region when EU capacity is unavailable, under defined controls.

h2. Customer requirem...
text:
h2. Summary
NordBank AB requested an exception to their EU-only data residency requirement to support disaster recovery testing and failover. Redwood Inference approved a *time-bounded* exception to route inference traffic to a US region when EU capacity is unavailable, under defined controls.

h2. Customer requirement
* Primary requirement: All inference requests and any associated logs must remain in EU regions (eu-west).
* Requested exception: Permit failover serving in US (us-east) during DR tests and during unplanned EU capacity incidents.

h2. Approved exception (scope and duration)
* Exception type: Out-of-region *serving* (failover routing)
* Approved regions: eu-west (primary), us-east (failover only)
* Duration: 2025-02-15 through 2025-05-31
* Data handling constraints:
 * No persistent prompt/response storage enabled for NordBank AB (logging disabled at the gateway for this...

Overview

Purpose: This playbook describes how Redwood orchestrates compliance evidence collection across RBAC/auth systems, audit logging, data residency boundaries, and retention policies. It defines owners, automation patterns, runbooks for pre-audit packaging and incident-led evidence collection, and the exception/risk workflow. It is intended for security engineers, platform owners, and compliance reviewers.

Scope
- Systems in-scope: auth-service (OIDC), rbac-manager (policy engine), audit-ingest (Kafka -> S3), audit-search (Elasticsearch), kms-yggdrasil (KMS/HSM integration), evidence-orchestrator (serverless job runner), compliance-indexer.
- Regions: us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Evidence types: audit records, configuration snapshots, access certification logs, key rotation proofs, data residency attestations, code-signing manifests, vulnerability scan reports.

Pri...

title: Customer data + logs retention policy (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)

# Purpose
This page defines the default retention and deletion timeframes for customer data and operational logs across Redwood Inference products (Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private).

# Definitions (quick)
- Customer content: prompts, files, and model outputs (request/response payloads).

text:
# Purpose
This page defines the default retention and deletion timeframes for customer data and operational logs across Redwood Inference products (Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private).

# Definitions (quick)
- Customer content: prompts, files, and model outputs (request/response payloads).
- Operational logs: API gateway logs, runtime logs, error logs, and request metadata (e.g., request id, status code, latency).
- Debug payload logging: optional feature that stores limited request/response payloads for troubleshooting.
- Audit logs: security-relevant actions (e.g., login, key creation, RBAC changes).

# Default retention windows
## Hosted API (Redwood-managed)
- Customer content (prompts/outputs): Not stored persistently by default.
- Debug payload logging (if explicitly enabled for an org): 7 days retention.
- Operational request logs (metadata, not payloads): 14 days retention.
- ...
